WHEN THE LIGHTS GO OUT: HOW CINEMAS ARE DYING, FROM LOUIE’S THX TO MAGNOLIA

This reflection looks at the slow death of cinemas through one person’s memories, from Louie’s THX in Makati to Magnolia and SM Cabanatuan. It traces how streaming, high ticket prices, and changing habits quietly hollowed out what used to be a shared experience. Along the way, it asks what we lose when moviegoing becomes a rare luxury instead of a normal part of life.

Magnolia appears to be closing its cinema.

I read it the other day. Took me a second to process why it bothered me so much. It bothered me because it feels less like an isolated event and more like a pattern. The kind of pattern I've been watching unfold for decades.

I lived through the 80s, the 90s, the 2000s. I watched things rise, peak, and vanish. And every single time, it happened so gradually that by the time you noticed, it was already over.

WHEN CINEMAS WERE ALIVE

There was a time when cinemas had their own economy. Their own people.

I remember the lagaristas. Most people have probably forgotten the word, but if you grew up in the 80s, you knew what they were. They were the men on bicycles who carried film reels from one cinema to the next. Theaters didn't own the reels. They shared them. So a lagarista would race across Manila on a bike, hauling heavy canisters of film on a tight schedule so that one cinema could start its 3pm screening after the 1pm show ended at another theater down the road. The timing had to be exact. If the lagarista was late, the audience got angry, and would shout at the projectionist. That was the system. Analog. Human. Held together by a guy on a bike pedaling through traffic.

The practice was so much a part of the culture that Mel Chionglo made an entire movie about it in 2000. "Lagarista," starring a young Piolo Pascual as a film biker.

Then there were the hand-painted movie billboards. Not printed. Painted. Full-color, larger-than-life faces of Fernando Poe Jr. and Nora Aunor, Vilma Santos, and many more towering over Banawe, Recto, Legarda, or the corner of EDSA and Aurora Boulevard. 

Sagmit Advertising Inc. was the company behind most of them, founded in 1960 by Eduardo Sagmit, a former accountant at the 20th Century Fox Manila office. Vic Delotavo became the most well-known painter, doing posters for Regal, Viva, and Seiko Films starting in the early 80s.  The billboards were constantly painted over and reused, so almost no archive of them exists. Eventually, the work dried up. An entire art form, gone. Parang wala lang. 

And there were double features. Two movies for the price of one ticket. You'd walk in at noon and come out at five. You didn't even have to like the second movie. You watched it because it was there and you already had a seat.

Lagaristas on bikes. Hand-painted billboards. Double features on a Sunday afternoon. That was what the cinema world looked like when I was growing up. Every part of it is gone now.

THE FIRST THX CINEMA IN MANILA

This reflection brought me back to my favorite ciname - Loui's THX Cinema.

It was on Amorsolo Street in Makati, inside the Mile Long Compound. The Rufino family built it in 1994. They had a history with cinemas going way back, to the old Manila movie houses on Escolta and Rizal Avenue, places like Capitol Theater, the State, the Avenue, the Ever.

Louie’s THX was different from anything else we had. It was the most high-tech cinema in Makati at the time, THX-certified, with sound and seats that felt years ahead of the malls. It felt like the future.

The last movie I watched there was Brendan Fraser's The Mummy. 1999. I remember walking out of that theater thinking the experience couldn't get much better than that.

Then one day, it was gone. The cinema couldn't compete with mall-based multiplexes. It eventually shut down and sat empty. In 2014, a Japanese filmmaker named Toshihiko Uriu converted the space into TIU Theater, a performing arts venue for urban poor children. And then in 2017, the government took over the entire Mile Long complex after a long legal battle with Sunvar Realty. Everyone was told to vacate.

I'VE SEEN THIS BEFORE

I grew up watching technologies peak and disappear.

Pagers. Little black boxes clipped to your belt that made you feel important. Cellphones showed up and pagers were done. One-hour photo labs, there were over 7,600 of them in the US at their peak in 1993. All gone. Floppy disks, Walkmans, VHS tapes, PDAs. PDAs felt like the future until the iPhone buried them in 2007.

Every time, the cycle was the same. Something useful reaches its peak. Something more convenient comes along. The old thing doesn't die right away. It lingers. Becomes nostalgic. Then one day, you look up and realize nobody's used it in years.

Kodak invented the digital camera but couldn't save itself from digital photography. Blockbuster had the chance to buy Netflix and laughed it off. Nokia owned the mobile phone market and couldn't pivot fast enough when smartphones arrived.

The thing that replaces you doesn't even have to be better. It just has to be more convenient.

But I digress...

PHILIPPINE CINEMAS ARE BLEEDING

The numbers here at home are rough.

A study by the Film Development Council of the Philippines in collaboration with De La Salle University found that streaming services have become the primary platform for 67% of Filipinos. Only 21% still regularly go to cinemas.

That gap is massive. And it keeps growing.

FDCP Chair Jose Javier Reyes has been raising the alarm for a while now. He pointed out that the audience has shrunk dramatically. Only those in the A, B, and a small portion of the C socioeconomic classes still show up. The D and E classes have almost entirely stopped going. A single ticket costs around P500. A family of four needs at least P1,500 just for one movie, not counting transpo or a snack. The cinema has become a luxury.

Reyes also flagged something else. The concept of "return viewers," the people who used to watch a favorite movie two, three times in theaters, has nearly disappeared. People watch once, then wait for it to land on streaming.

Wala na yung dating excitement. Yung pipila ka pa sa ticket booth, yung mag-aaway kayo ng tropa mo kung saan uupo.

The pandemic made all of this worse. Three years of lockdowns changed habits for good. People got used to watching from home. K-dramas, foreign films, Netflix originals. The couch won.

In Alabang, Festival Mall's original cinemas opened in 1998 with six screens and eventually grew to ten. Now only two or three screens still operate on any given day. Commercenter's four-screen cinema in Filinvest City closed in 2025. That's fourteen screens gone from one city alone.

Even here in Cabanatuan, you can feel it. SM Cinema isn't immune. The slump is visible. Fewer people lining up, more empty seats on a Friday night. If a city like this is feeling it, the smaller towns don't stand a chance.

And now, Magnolia.

IT'S HAPPENING EVERYWHERE

This isn't just us.

AMC, the largest cinema chain in the world with over 850 locations, has closed 213 theaters in the past five years. In February, the company said it plans to keep closing more than it opens. Their Q4 2025 foot traffic dropped nearly 10%. From 62 million moviegoers down to 56.3 million. They posted a net loss of $632.4 million for 2025.

Globally, cinema ticket sales fell 8.8% in 2024. That's 500 million fewer tickets sold compared to 2023. Total attendance is now sitting at just 68% of 2019 levels. We haven't bounced back. We've plateaued. And the plateau is way below where we used to be.

In the US, the percentage of people who go to the movies at least once a month has dropped from 39% in 2019 to just 17% in 2025. Among those going less, 66% said cost was the main reason. Almost half said they'd rather wait and watch at home on a streaming service they already pay for.

Singapore had it rough in 2025. One journalist called it a "cinema-pocalypse." The Projector, an 11-year-old indie cinema beloved by film fans, suddenly shut down in August. Two weeks later, Cathay Cineplexes, with 87 years of history, ceased operations entirely. Filmgarde and WE Cinemas also closed. Four cinema operators gone in one country in roughly a year.

In the US, LOOK Dine-In Cinemas, which tried to reinvent the theater experience with food and premium seating, still had to close three LA locations in early 2026.

If even the reinvention model can't survive, what does that tell you?

WHAT STREAMING ACTUALLY REPLACED

Streaming didn't just replace cinemas. It replaced the reason to leave your house for entertainment.

When I was growing up, going to the movies was an event. You planned it. You got dressed. You argued with your friends about what to watch. The screen was massive, the sound was deafening, and for two hours, you were inside someone else's world.

Now you can get 80% of that experience on a 55-inch TV with a home theater and a Netflix subscription. And you don't have to wear pants.

The remaining 20%, the massive screen, the collective gasps, the feeling of strangers laughing together in the dark, that part can't be replicated. But the market has decided that 20% isn't worth the price of a ticket anymore.

Reyes admitted as much. The change was already happening as early as 2017 in the Philippines. The pandemic just sped up something that was already in motion.

And the younger generation, the ones who should be the future audience, grew up with content on their phones. They don't have the same attachment to the theater experience. Watching a movie on a laptop with earbuds is normal to them. They never experienced Louie's THX. They never sat through a double feature. They never saw a lagarista pull up on a bike with the next reel.

FOR CINEMA ONLY

I still watch movies in theaters. Every now and then. It's my small way of helping the industry, especially when something comes out that isn't another 
kabit-themed
 movie.

My daughter and I have this thing where we tag certain films as "for cinema only." The ones where the screen has to be big. The ones where the sound has to shake the seat. The ones that would lose something if you watched them on a laptop. We treat those like events, because they are.

The last movie I actually watched in a cinema wasn't even in the Philippines. I was in Canada, and my brother-in-law invited me to watch Avatar: Fire and Ash. On Boxing Day. The theater was packed. Every seat taken. I'll be honest, it had been a long time since I'd seen a cinema that full. I haven't seen that in the Philippines in a while, though I'll admit that might be because I'm confined to the province. Cabanatuan isn't exactly Metro Manila.

We watched it in 4DX. I'd never tried it before. It's this multi-sensory cinema technology developed by CJ 4DPlex. The seats move in sync with the action on screen. There's wind, water mist, scents, snow, lightning effects, vibrations. Over 20 different environmental effects built into the experience. They mostly use it for blockbusters and horror films, and Avatar was the perfect movie for it. When Pandora's oceans hit the screen, you felt the spray. When something flew overhead, the seat tilted with it.

That's a cinema movie. You don't watch that on a phone. You don't stream that on a laptop. You sit in that chair and you let the room take over.

But that's the thing. I had to be in another country to have that experience. And the fact that I remember it so clearly tells you how rare my cinema-going experience has become.

THE GRAVEYARD OF THINGS THAT PEAKED

I think about this more than I probably should.

The pager peaked in the 90s. The one-hour photo lab peaked in 93. VHS peaked just before DVD showed up. DVD peaked just before Blu-ray arrived. Blu-ray barely had time to breathe before streaming buried it. Each one, at its peak, felt permanent.

Cinemas felt permanent too. They've been around for over a century. They survived radio, television, home video, cable, and piracy. Every time, people said this would be the thing that killed cinemas. Every time, cinemas adapted.

But streaming might be different. Not because the content is better. Because the convenience gap is too wide. And because an entire generation built its viewing habits during a pandemic when theaters were closed for three years.

The AMC CEO talks about a strong 2026 lineup, with films like Spider-Man and the next Moana and Dune installments that should bring people back. And maybe they will. Blockbusters still draw crowds. But blockbusters are seasonal. The everyday moviegoer, the one who went on a random Tuesday night just because, that person is gone.

WHAT WE ACTUALLY LOSE

When a cinema closes, we lose more than a screen.

We lose a place where strangers shared something together. Where teenagers went on first dates. Where kids watched their first superhero movie and walked out feeling like they could do anything.

Sa Pilipinas, cinemas were one of the few places where class didn't matter as much. You bought a ticket, you sat in the dark, you watched the same movie as everyone else. The MMFF used to be described as "the people's festival," the one time ordinary workers would take their kids to the mall to see their idols on the big screen. That's fading.

Director Jun Robles Lana said it well. Cinema has gone from a shared national experience to a luxury for the middle class.

And once something becomes a luxury, it stops being part of the culture. It becomes niche. And niche things are the ones that end up next to pagers, floppy disks, and one-hour photo labs.

I don't know if cinemas will disappear completely. They probably won't. There will always be IMAX screens and 4DX rooms and premium experiences for those who can afford them. But the neighborhood cinema, the one inside the mall where you'd catch a movie on impulse, the one at Magnolia or Festival Mall or Commercenter, the SM Cinema here in Cabanatuan where the seats are half-empty on a weekend, those are the ones that are dying.

I watched Brendan Fraser fight a mummy in the first THX cinema in Manila. The building belongs to the government now. The screen is gone. The seats are gone. The lagaristas are gone. The hand-painted billboards are gone. The double features are gone.

And nobody noticed when it happened.

As someone who grew up watching movies in dark, crowded theaters, I feel a specific kind of grief watching this part of our lives fade. Call it a habit, a ritual, a small piece of culture we shared without thinking too hard about it. 

So while I still can, my quiet promise is simple: I’ll keep choosing the cinema when it matters, I’ll avoid piracy, and I’ll pay for the stories that deserve that giant screen. 

Tara, nood tayo ng sine. Bring your friends, your family, your workmates. Kung kaya pa, tulungan natin na mabuhay itong industriya.

🟥 DMW DECLARES PERSIAN GULF, STRAIT OF HORMUZ, AND GULF OF OMAN AS WARLIKE OPERATIONS AREAS — FILIPINO SEAFARERS CAN NOW LEGALLY REFUSE TO SAIL

On Saturday night, March 8, Migrant Workers Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac signed DMW Advisory No. 11, Series of 2026, officially designating the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas."

What that means in practical terms: any Filipino crew member assigned to a vessel operating in those waters now has the legal right to decline the assignment.

This is not a suggestion. It is a formal government advisory built on a resolution from the Warlike Operations Area Committee of the International Bargaining Forum, the global body that negotiates conditions for commercial seafarers. The IBF classification triggers specific provisions under international maritime labor agreements, including enhanced insurance coverage, additional compensation for war-risk sailing, and the crew's right of refusal.

🟥 THE SCOPE

The three designated zones cover virtually every major petroleum shipping route in the Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz alone accounts for roughly 20 to 21 percent of global daily oil consumption — about 21 million barrels per day transit through that chokepoint, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The Persian Gulf connects Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE. The Gulf of Oman sits between Iran and Oman, serving as the eastern entrance to the Strait.

Filipino seafarers are among the largest maritime labor forces in the world. The Philippines supplies an estimated 25 percent of the global merchant shipping workforce. According to the Philippine Overseas Labor Office, there were approximately 401,826 sea-based OFWs deployed in 2024. A significant number of those serve on oil tankers, bulk carriers, and container ships that routinely transit the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz.

🟥 WHAT THE ADVISORY ACTUALLY REQUIRES

In his video message Saturday evening, Cacdac laid it out directly: "Filipino crew members, especially ship owners employing Filipino personnel, should avoid operating in that region. If they must go there, they must grant Filipino crew members the autonomy to decline sailing and steer clear of these dangerous waters."

The advisory requires shipowners employing Filipino seafarers to do three things: first, inform crew members about the warlike operations designation before any transit through the affected zones; second, respect the right of any Filipino crew member to refuse sailing into those waters without penalty or contract termination; and third, comply with enhanced safety and compensation protocols mandated under the IBF framework.

The DMW also announced it would engage with shipowners, maritime labor groups, and international bodies to coordinate implementation. For Filipino seafarers already aboard vessels in or near the designated zones, the advisory's protections apply immediately.

🟥 WHY THIS IS DIFFERENT FROM THE GENERAL REPATRIATION

The Philippine government has already been managing repatriation of land-based OFWs from the Middle East since the US-Israel strikes on Iran began in late February. As of early March, over 1,100 OFWs had requested repatriation, and the DMW had also paused new deployments of land-based workers to the region.

But seafarers operate under a completely different framework. They are governed by international maritime labor conventions, not standard overseas employment contracts. Their vessels may be flagged under third-country registries — Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands — meaning the chain of legal authority runs through flag states, shipowners (often multinational), manning agencies in Manila, and international bargaining bodies.

The DMW advisory cuts through that layered jurisdictional tangle by invoking the IBF's warlike operations designation. It provides Filipino crew members with a recognized international legal basis to refuse assignment, backed by both Philippine government policy and global maritime labor agreements. Without this classification, a seafarer who refused to sail into the Gulf could face contract penalties or blacklisting by the manning agency.

🟥 THE TIMING

The advisory comes as Middle East hostilities continue to escalate. Since late February, the United States and Israel have conducted strikes against Iranian military and nuclear targets. Iran has responded with missile attacks on Israeli territory and US military installations in the Gulf states. Airspace closures have disrupted commercial aviation across the region. Oil prices have spiked globally, with the Philippines — which imports over 90 percent of its fuel — bracing for diesel price increases of up to P20 per liter this week.

For Filipino seafarers, the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz are not abstract geopolitical flashpoints. These are the waters they physically transit, sometimes multiple times per voyage, aboard vessels carrying the crude oil and petroleum products that fuel the global economy.

The last time the IBF classified a major waterway as a warlike operations area affecting Filipino seafarers at this scale was during the height of Somali piracy in the Gulf of Aden. That designation similarly triggered enhanced protections for crew members, including bonus pay, additional insurance, and the right to refuse transit.

🟥 LAWYER FERDINAND TOPACIO ADMITS LUSTING AFTER CONGRESSWOMAN SARAH ELAGO ON LIVE RADIO, FACES DISBARMENT THREAT

While defending Quezon City Rep. Bong Suntay for his sexualized remarks about Anne Curtis during an impeachment hearing, pro-Duterte lawyer Ferdinand Topacio went on his DWIZ radio show on March 5 and made it worse.

He admitted he once lusted after Gabriela Women's Party Rep. Sarah Elago.

His exact words: "Ako dati, pinagnasahan ko rin si Sarah Elago, e. Naamoy ko noong hearing namin sa DOJ." Translation: he said he desired Elago after smelling her during a Department of Justice hearing.

Then he turned on actress Jasmine Curtis-Smith, Anne's sister, who had publicly criticized Suntay. He asked why she posts bikini photos online. "Isn't that for you to be desired?" he said. He dragged in other celebrities, naming Ivana Alawi. "Why do they post pictures? Isn't it for the views? That's just being straightforward."

He was not done. He dismissed the entire backlash against Suntay as hypocrisy. "He's a man. Let's not kid ourselves. Men have desires. We are wired for that. Let's not be hypocrites."

When asked if he cared about the public anger, Topacio said he did not. "Magalit na kayo kung magagalit kayo sa akin. I don't care."

🟥 WHO IS FERDINAND TOPACIO

Topacio is a well-known figure in Philippine legal and political circles. He has represented clients tied to the Duterte administration and has built a media presence through his DWIZ radio program "Yes... Yes... Yo! Topacio."

He is a member of the Integrated Bar of the Philippines and is bound by the Code of Professional Responsibility and Accountability, the Supreme Court's ethical code for lawyers. That code requires members of the bar to avoid conduct that discredits the legal profession.

The remarks aired during the first week of National Women's Month.

🟥 MAKABAYAN BLOC FIRES BACK

Reps. Sarah Elago, ACT Teachers Rep. Antonio Tinio, and Kabataan Rep. Renee Co issued a joint statement condemning Topacio's remarks as "vile, misogynistic, and deeply degrading attacks."

"Rather than address the substance of the criticism, that it is wrong to use a woman as a prop in an analogy to defend an accused murderer's ally, Topacio went straight for the gutter," the bloc said.

They called his remarks "harassment" and "abuse dressed up in a suit and a law degree."

The bloc directly invoked the logic of sexual violence. "This is victim blaming. This is the logic of rape culture, that a woman's appearance, choices, and body are open invitations for objectification, and that she forfeits her right to dignity and to speak the moment she dares to be visible."

They called on the Integrated Bar of the Philippines to investigate Topacio for "conduct unbecoming of a lawyer." They also called on radio station DWIZ and its management to "review and account for the content aired" during his program timeslot. And they urged the House Committee on Women and Gender Equality to act.

🟥 GABRIELA WEIGHS DISBARMENT

The Gabriela Women's Party issued a separate statement on March 6 condemning Topacio.

Gabriela said his statements were "misogynistic and malicious" and accused him of a long track record of red-tagging and attacking women. The group said it is studying possible legal actions, including a disbarment complaint before the Supreme Court.

A disbarment case, if filed, would be reviewed by the Supreme Court's Office of the Bar Confidant and could result in Topacio losing his license to practice law.

Gabriela also questioned whether individuals like Topacio "should be allowed to use the legal profession to demean women who speak out against abuse and corruption."

🟥 JASMINE CURTIS RESPONDS

Jasmine Curtis-Smith posted a video clip of Topacio's remarks on her Facebook Stories. Her caption: "DUGYOT KA!" Roughly translated: "You're gross."

Her mother Carmen Curtis and other celebrities had already condemned Suntay earlier in the week. Jasmine's own sister, Anne Curtis, rejected Suntay's non-apology on March 7, calling it exactly that and confirming she is seeking legal advice.

🟥 THE WIDER PATTERN

Topacio's remarks did not happen in isolation. They are the second incident in the same week involving a public figure making sexualized comments about women in a professional or institutional setting.

Suntay made his original remarks about Anne Curtis during a House Committee on Justice hearing on March 3, while defending Vice President Sara Duterte's impeachment case. He described feeling desire after seeing Curtis at a hotel and argued he could not be criminally liable for his imagination.

The House ordered an ethics investigation into Suntay on March 5. Nine women filed criminal complaints against him at the Ombudsman on March 6, citing the Safe Spaces Act, the Magna Carta of Women, and the Code of Conduct for Public Officials.

Separately, Senator Jinggoy Estrada drew backlash on March 6 for asking American basketball player Elizabeth Means during a Senate hearing whether she has a Filipino boyfriend.

Three incidents involving three different men in three different institutional settings, all during Women's Month.

As of March 8, the Integrated Bar of the Philippines has not issued a public statement on Topacio's remarks.

🟥 MARCOS ORDERS FOUR-DAY WORKWEEK FOR ALL GOVERNMENT OFFICES STARTING MARCH 9

Starting Sunday, every government office in the Philippines operates on a different schedule.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. signed Memorandum Circular 114 on Friday, directing all national government agencies, government-owned or controlled corporations, and local government units to adopt a four-day onsite work arrangement. The fifth day shifts to work-from-home or gets dropped entirely under a compressed workweek format.

The order takes effect March 9 and stays until Marcos personally revokes it.

Executive Secretary Ralph Recto signed the circular. The Civil Service Commission will issue the implementing guidelines for flexible work arrangements, attendance verification, and performance monitoring.

🟥 WHO IS EXEMPT

Police. Firefighters. Emergency responders. Any agency providing frontline public services.

The order explicitly carves out offices that handle emergency or critical functions. Everyone else in the executive branch falls under the new arrangement.

The policy does not apply to the private sector. George Barcelon, chairman emeritus of the Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry, said during a One News panel that some private companies might voluntarily follow suit, but acknowledged it would not be feasible across all industries.

That distinction has already drawn public frustration. Reactions on social media pointed out that private sector workers — particularly minimum wage earners who commute daily — face the same fuel cost pressure but receive no equivalent relief.

🟥 THE ENERGY CONSERVATION PACKAGE

The four-day workweek is one piece of a broader austerity directive.

MC 114 requires all government agencies to cut electricity consumption and fuel expenses by 10 to 20 percent. Every department must designate an energy conservation officer, cooperate with government energy audits, and submit monthly consumption data.

Non-essential government travel is now temporarily banned. That includes study tours, team-building events, and in-person meetings that could be conducted online. The circular specifies that travel without clear justification of urgency, operational necessity, or direct programmatic relevance counts as unnecessary.

🟥 THE TRIGGER

Marcos issued the directive in a video address on Friday, hours before the DOE confirmed the largest single-week fuel price hike in the country's deregulated history. Diesel was projected to rise P21 to P23 per liter on March 10. Gasoline P11 to P13. Kerosene P33 to P35.

The country imports over 90% of its oil from the Middle East. The ongoing US-Israel conflict with Iran has disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and sent global crude past $90 per barrel. The Department of Energy has warned prices will keep climbing for weeks.

Senator Sherwin Gatchalian had floated the four-day workweek idea earlier in the week as part of broader energy conservation proposals. Marcos initially said he would study the idea. By Friday, it was signed into policy.

🟥 ELECTRICITY RATES ARE NEXT

The same day Marcos signed MC 114, the Energy Regulatory Commission issued its own warning.

The ERC, in coordination with the Independent Electricity Market Operator of the Philippines, ran simulations on what rising international coal, oil, and liquefied natural gas prices would do to domestic power costs. Their finding: higher fuel costs could push prices in the Wholesale Electricity Spot Market upward as generators pass costs through their market offers.

The Philippines' energy mix leans heavily on coal and LNG. The ERC said the power sector remains particularly sensitive to global commodity price swings, and reminded distribution utilities and generation companies that existing market safeguards — including price caps and secondary price caps — remain in place.

For households, that means the government is bracing for a power bill increase on top of the pump price surge. No specific figures were released.

🟥 THE FUEL SUBSIDY QUESTION

Marcos mentioned during his video address that the government plans to introduce fuel subsidies to support sectors most affected by the price hikes. He did not provide details — no peso amount, no timeline, no mechanism for distribution.

The government's fiscal room for fuel subsidies is constrained. The 2025 budget deficit came in at P1.58 trillion, overshooting the target by P60 billion. 🟥 NINE WOMEN FILE CRIMINAL COMPLAINT AGAINST REP. SUNTAY AT OMBUDSMAN; HOUSE ORDERS ETHICS PROBE

[UPDATE to Suntay-Anne Curtis Sexism Case] | March 7, 2026

Four days after Quezon City Rep. Bong Suntay publicly described a sexual desire for actress Anne Curtis on the floor of a congressional hearing, the legal and institutional walls are closing in from multiple directions simultaneously.

On Friday, March 6, nine women — including members of the World March of Women-Pilipinas (WMW-Pilipinas) and the Women's Legal and Human Rights Bureau — filed a formal complaint-affidavit at the Office of the Ombudsman against the lawmaker.

Jean Enriquez, national coordinator of WMW-Pilipinas, personally submitted the letter-complaint to Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla, calling for the investigation and imposition of appropriate penalties.

"It is evident that he is mimicking his idol, former president Rodrigo Duterte, whose sexist behavior and violence against women we have endured for a decade," Enriquez said. "Congressman Suntay has reignited trauma for many of us."

🟥 FOUR LAWS, ONE COMPLAINT

The complaint cites four legal frameworks Suntay allegedly violated.

The first is Republic Act 11313, the Safe Spaces Act, which prohibits and criminalizes sexist and degrading remarks in public spaces. The second is RA 9710, the Magna Carta of Women, which obligates government officials to not discriminate against women or allow violations of their rights. The third is RA 6713, the Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials.

The fourth is the Code of Professional Responsibility and Accountability, or CPRA — the Supreme Court's ethical code for lawyers. Because Suntay is a practicing lawyer, the complainants argued he violated the provision requiring lawyers to avoid scandalous behavior that discredits the legal profession.

The complainants called the remarks a "public crime." The Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) had already labeled them "a declaration of predatory desire directed at a woman, made openly, by a person who holds public trust and authority" — in a statement issued March 4 describing the words as "a public act of sexual objectification" bearing the hallmarks of gender-based sexual harassment under RA 11313.

🟥 THE ETHICS PROBE

Two days before the Ombudsman complaint was filed, the House of Representatives had already moved.

On the evening of Wednesday, March 5, at the close of plenary session, San Juan Representative Ysabel Maria Zamora moved to refer the matter to the House Committee on Ethics and Privileges. The motion authorized the committee to "thoroughly assess" Suntay's actions and statements, compel him to appear before the committee, and report findings and recommendations back to the full House.

No one objected. The motion passed.

House Committee on Women and Gender Equality Chair Ann Matibag had earlier delivered a privilege speech that laid the ground for the probe. "Women are not a joke, nor are they mere fantasies," Matibag said on the floor. "The dignity of women should never be treated as a source of ridicule within Congress."

Suntay said he was accepting the ethics complaint. "If they want to file an ethics complaint, if they want to judge me, the person that I am, based from one particular statement, I respect it," he said.

He maintained his remarks carried no malicious intent. "The analogy may have been in poor taste and taken out of context. But if you read the context, there was nothing malicious in it."

🟥 THE GASLIGHTING ACCUSATION

The nine complainants at the Ombudsman explicitly challenged Suntay's characterization of his own apology.

His apology — issued March 4 — expressed regret only for those who were "offended," while defending the remarks as an analogy and insisting there was no malice. The complainants called this "gaslighting." They argued that framing "inappropriateness" as subjective — something that depends on how an individual received it — dismisses the real harm caused to women and re-traumatizes survivors of gender-based violence.

"The apology and the removal of his remarks from the committee records do not absolve him of responsibility," Jelen Paclarin of the Women's Legal and Human Rights Bureau said.

🟥 ILOILO WEIGHS IN, ANNE CURTIS HOLDS

The League of Municipalities of the Philippines — Iloilo Chapter (LMP-Iloilo) released a statement on Saturday condemning the remarks and calling public officials to account.

"Comments that could belittle, objectify, or undermine the dignity of women contradict the principles of gender equality and respectful dialogue that our institutions strive to uphold," the group said.

The statement underscored the timing — the controversy erupted during National Women's Month.

Anne Curtis, for her part, had already rejected Suntay's apology on March 7 with a public statement. "I do not accept your non-apology," she wrote. She confirmed she is seeking legal advice — a decision that may add a separate private action to the growing stack of institutional complaints already in motion.

Suntay now faces exposure on three parallel tracks: a criminal complaint before the Ombudsman, a formal ethics investigation by the House, and the possibility of legal action by Curtis herself.

He authored Quezon City's Gender and Development Code and its anti-harassment ordinance — a fact he has cited repeatedly in his own defense.

🟥 SENATOR JINGGOY ESTRADA ASKS GILAS WOMEN PROSPECT ELIZABETH MEANS IF SHE HAS A FILIPINO BOYFRIEND DURING NATURALIZATION HEARING — DEFENDS IT AS "NOTHING MALICIOUS"

March 7, 2026

The hearing on March 3 was supposed to be about citizenship.

Elizabeth Jeanette Means, an American basketball forward and Gilas Pilipinas Women's prospect, appeared before the Senate Committee on Justice and Human Rights to advance her naturalization under Senate Bill 1594. The committee — chaired by Senator Francis "Kiko" Pangilinan — was examining whether she genuinely intended to make the Philippines her permanent home.

Senator Jinggoy Estrada had a different line of questioning.

After asking Means where she lived and what Filipino food she liked, Estrada asked: "Do you have a Filipino boyfriend?"

Means replied, "Not yet po." Estrada then added: "Maybe Senator Pangilinan might want to apply." He followed with: "Sorry kay Sharon."

Pangilinan, who is married to actress Sharon Cuneta, responded with a laugh: "Magagalit yung commander."

The exchange was livestreamed. It spread quickly.

🟥 WHO ELIZABETH MEANS IS

Means is a 6-foot forward from Folsom, California. She completed her NCAA Division II career at Westminster University in Utah, then spent her first professional season in Ecuador. Coach Anton Altamirano of the Gilas Women's program approached her agent and brought her to the Philippines, where she has been based since late 2025.

At the Senate hearing alongside her was Maodo Malick Diouf — the UAAP Most Valuable Player from the University of the Philippines — who is seeking naturalization for Gilas Pilipinas 3x3. Both athletes expressed that they already felt at home in the Philippines during the hearing.

Means' case was filed under Senate Bill 1594, authored by Senator Pia Cayetano. The Senate committee is one step in a formal legislative process that also requires House passage and presidential approval.

She was there to answer questions about her commitment to the country. The question she was asked instead was about her romantic life.

🟥 THE BACKLASH

Social media reaction was swift and pointed.

Filmmaker Jun Lana posted on X: "When will our leaders realize that their casual sexism is a national embarrassment?"

Ceej Tantengco-Malolos, founder of sports advocacy group Go Hard Girls, called it inappropriate and out of place — a senator asking an athlete about her personal life during what should have been a formal assessment of her commitment to Philippine citizenship. The question, she said, was irrelevant to anything the committee was tasked to determine.

Other online reactions called the remark rude and disgusting, with several users pointing out that Estrada knew Pangilinan was married — and to one of the most recognizable women in Philippine entertainment history.

One widely shared comment put it plainly: "Every day, may entry sa Women's Month ang mga public officials, na para bang may challenge for the most insensitive, sexist politician."

🟥 ESTRADA'S STATEMENT

On Friday evening, Estrada broke his silence through a formal statement.

"It may not be widely known, but Senator Pangilinan's wife, Ms. Sharon Cuneta, is a dear friend of mine, and our friendship goes back many years — long before I entered politics," he said.

He offered a procedural explanation for the boyfriend question: "Part of the Committee's responsibility is to understand whether applicants intend to truly make the Philippines their home — whether by settling here permanently or even establishing a family in the future."

"There was absolutely nothing malicious in the question," he added. "It was asked in a light and conversational manner during the proceedings."

He described the exchange as "friendly banter" directed at his colleague Pangilinan, not at Means.

🟥 THE TIMING

This is the second incident in the first week of Women's Month involving a male lawmaker making remarks about a woman during formal proceedings.

On the same day as the Means hearing — March 3 — Quezon City Representative Bong Suntay made headlines for describing a sexual fantasy about actress Anne Curtis during a House committee hearing on the Sara Duterte impeachment complaints. Suntay's remarks drew a formal complaint to the Office of the Ombudsman, a House ethics referral, and a public statement from Anne Curtis rejecting his non-apology.

Both incidents occurred inside formal legislative chambers. Both involved a male lawmaker directing unsolicited personal or sexualized commentary at a woman. Both happened in the same week of March — which the Philippine government officially designates as Women's Month.

Suntay's remarks were eventually struck from the record by a 13-2 committee vote. In the Estrada case, no committee objection was recorded. No formal complaint has been filed as of Saturday.

🟥 WHO JINGGOY ESTRADA IS

Estrada is a sitting senator and the son of former President Joseph Estrada. He is a member of the Senate Committee on Justice and Human Rights — the same committee that handled Means' naturalization hearing.

As of this writing, he is also under a precautionary hold departure order issued February 10 by Manila Regional Trial Court Branch 52. The order was issued in connection with a plunder case filed by the National Bureau of Investigation over alleged irregularities in P355 million worth of flood control projects in Bulacan. His co-respondents include former DPWH Secretary Manuel Bonoan and three other former DPWH officials. The charges include plunder under Republic Act 7080, violations of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, and direct bribery. Estrada has denied all allegations.

His counter-affidavit in the case was due March 9, 2026.

🟥 DIESEL HEADING TO P80 OR BEYOND: THE RECORD MARCH 10 FUEL HIKE, PNOC'S RESERVE BID, AND THE SPR BILL THAT COULD HAVE PREVENTED THIS

[UPDATE to Oil Crisis Coverage] | March 7, 2026

Ten weeks of consecutive diesel price hikes. The 11th arrives Tuesday with a number that dwarfs every adjustment that came before it.

Latest industry estimates put the March 10 diesel increase at P21 to P23 per liter. Gasoline is expected to rise by P11 to P13 per liter. Kerosene could jump P33 to P35 per liter.

The Department of Energy has framed those as minimums. DOE Oil Industry Management Bureau Director Rino Abad confirmed diesel could breach P80 per liter if oil companies apply the full adjustment without staggering. The DOE's worst-case projection: diesel could go past P90 per liter if the Middle East conflict drags on.

Diesel in the capital region was trading at P60.50 to P63.19 per liter this week. On Tuesday, that could be P80.

🟥 WHY THIS ADJUSTMENT IS DIFFERENT

The 11th straight diesel hike is driven by the US-Israel conflict with Iran, but amplified by a new variable: China.

Beijing has asked its refiners to halt new export contracts for refined fuel. The Philippines imports roughly 30% of its diesel from China. That supply is now uncertain.

The rest — approximately 90% of total crude — comes from the Middle East. Ships through the Strait of Hormuz, through which about one-fifth of global oil supply moves, face active disruption risk. BPI lead economist Emilio Neri has said a prolonged blockade could push Brent crude to $100 to $120 per barrel.

The TRAIN Law originally contained an automatic safeguard: excise taxes on petroleum would suspend if global crude reached $80 per barrel for three consecutive months. That provision expired after 2020. Any tax relief now requires a fresh act of Congress.

🟥 PNOC'S FIVE-DAY BUFFER

The DOE has directed the Philippine National Oil Company to procure at least one million barrels of diesel as an emergency stockpile.

At current domestic consumption of roughly 200,000 barrels a day, one million barrels covers approximately five days of supply. Abad said the volume could scale to three million barrels — about 15 days — depending on how the situation develops.

PNOC would source from South Korea, Japan, Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia, and would sell the diesel at cost to domestic oil companies. "At best, PNOC may sell the fuel at cost. It will simply recover the procurement expenses and distribute the supply to domestic oil companies," Abad said.

Five to fifteen days. That is the government's emergency buffer.

🟥 THE SPR BILL THAT ARRIVED LATE

Senate President Tito Sotto filed Senate Bill 1934 on March 4. The Philippine Strategic Petroleum Reserve Act would establish a state-managed reserve covering at least 90 days of national consumption — crude oil, diesel, gasoline, jet fuel, and LPG stored across regional facilities nationwide.

"At present, the country does not maintain a comprehensive, state-managed strategic petroleum reserve capable of cushioning prolonged supply interruptions," Sotto said in the bill's explanatory note. "While private oil companies maintain commercial inventories, these are not designed nor mandated to serve as national emergency reserves."

The Philippines has no SPR. Private company stocks amount to roughly two to three months of inventory — commercial buffers, not a government-controlled strategic reserve.

SB 1934 is not a new idea. It is a revival of one that should have moved years ago.

Stansberry Research analyst Brian Tycangco, who covers Asia energy markets, wrote on X on Saturday: "The Philippines is literally getting screwed in this global energy crisis, and it is largely due to a lack of preparation. President Duterte's administration proposed building a Strategic Petroleum Reserve back in 2022 when oil prices started climbing on the heels of Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Inaction by the Department of Energy and lack of interest from the majority of lawmakers have left the country fully exposed to the chaos unfolding in the global energy markets today."

Tycangco put a figure on the missed window: a 60-day reserve, built when oil prices were falling between 2023 and 2025, would have cost P100 billion to P110 billion. The 2025 budget alone contained more than P700 billion in congressional budget insertions.

🟥 WHAT IS SPREADING DOWNSTREAM

Montenegro Shipping Lines announced fare increases of 10% to 25% on all routes effective March 23, citing a nearly 40% rise in fuel costs since the Middle East conflict escalated. The increase covers passenger tickets and rolling cargo across every Montenegro route nationwide.

BPI's Neri has warned that rising energy costs could push headline inflation toward 4%. The BSP has cut policy rates by 225 basis points since August 2024. A sustained oil shock — particularly if WTI remains near $80 per barrel through June — could restrict the BSP's ability to cut further.

🟥 WHERE IT STANDS NOW

SB 1934 has been filed. It is in committee. The emergency powers bill giving Marcos authority to cut fuel excise taxes is not yet law. The staggering arrangement between DOE and oil companies is voluntary — there is no legal mechanism forcing oil firms to absorb losses on adjustments they are legally entitled to implement.

On March 10, oil companies can apply the full P21 to P23 diesel increase in one adjustment. The SPR bill — if it ever passes — covers the crisis after this one.

🟥 DOE ORDERS ALL OIL COMPANIES AND GAS STATIONS TO COMPLY WITH FUEL PRICING RULES, DEPLOYS PNP FOR NATIONWIDE MONITORING

[UPDATE to Fuel Crisis Coverage] | March 7, 2026

A gas station in Tagum City, Davao del Norte, got caught hiking diesel by P8.35 per liter before the scheduled price adjustment date.

That is one of the first confirmed violations since the Department of Energy issued a formal directive on March 7 ordering every oil company, gasoline station, and downstream industry participant in the Philippines to strictly comply with existing fuel pricing rules and anti-hoarding regulations.

The order came directly under the directive of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.

🟥 THE ORDER

No more unscheduled price increases. No unauthorized adjustments outside established pricing windows. That is the core of the DOE directive.

Oil companies have been told to make sure every station they own or that operates under their brand follows the order, including any authorized staggered price adjustments.

Gasoline stations are now prohibited from dispensing fuel into drums, gallons, or other containers. All fuel must go directly into the vehicle tank unless otherwise provided by existing rules.

The DOE specifically called out "botebote" sales and the filling of drums or gallons for the purpose of stockpiling. If those acts are being used to facilitate hoarding or illegal resale, they fall under the enforcement net.

🟥 WHAT THEY FOUND IN TAGUM CITY

DOE Mindanao Field Office teams, working alongside PNP units in Tagum City and Davao City, conducted inspections starting Thursday, March 5.

At one retail station in Tagum City, the posted pump price of diesel had jumped from P64.85 per liter to P73.20 — an increase of P8.35. The station had implemented the price adjustment before the prescribed schedule.

The DOE team ordered the station to revert to the correct price level immediately.

🟥 THE PNP AND DILG DEPLOYMENT

This is not just the DOE working alone.

The department has formally asked the Department of the Interior and Local Government and the Philippine National Police to help with inspections and enforcement at gasoline stations nationwide.

Joint DOE-PNP monitoring teams are now conducting inspections across Mindanao, with expanded coverage scheduled to begin Saturday.

DOE field offices in Luzon and Visayas have also ramped up on-site inspections. The Cebu field office had already flagged irregularities earlier in the week — stations that unilaterally raised prices and operators who allegedly refused to sell fuel to motorists, a tactic used to preserve stock for future sale at higher prices.

🟥 THE LEGAL BASIS

Two laws anchor the enforcement.

Republic Act 7581, the Price Act, classifies hoarding and profiteering of kerosene and LPG as illegal acts of price manipulation. Violators face fines and imprisonment.

Presidential Decree 1865 provides the framework to penalize supply disruptions, short selling, adulteration, and overpricing of petroleum products. Under this decree, the government can immediately confiscate petroleum products found to be part of illegal stockpiling.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin did not leave room for interpretation: "We will not tolerate any attempt to take advantage of the current situation at the expense of Filipino consumers."

Violations of the DOE directive can result in revocation of the Certificate of Compliance or License to Operate, suspension or cancellation of business permits, and criminal penalties under existing laws.

🟥 THE PRICING PICTURE GOING INTO NEXT WEEK

The next regular fuel price adjustment is set for Tuesday, March 10.

DOE Oil Industry Management Bureau Director Rino Abad said on Saturday that the minimum projected increases are P19 per liter for diesel, P9 for gasoline, and P31 for kerosene. Those are minimums — they do not include additional premiums or insurance costs driven by the Middle East conflict.

Those figures are higher than the industry estimates earlier in the week, which had pegged diesel at P15.33 per liter and gasoline at P7.09.

The DOE published the allowable pump price ranges for March 6 to 9: RON 91 gasoline sits between P49 and P64.70 per liter. Diesel ranges from P49 to P66.59. Kerosene runs from P78.90 to P99.89.

Any station posting prices outside those ranges before March 10 is violating the directive.

🟥 THE REPORTING CHANNELS

The DOE is asking the public to document suspected violations with photos or videos, including the gasoline station name, exact location, date and time, posted prices, and receipts.

Complaints can be filed through government hotline 8888, the DOE Consumer Welfare and Promotion Office at 8840-2267, official DOE Facebook Messenger, or by email at mcwpo@doe.gov.ph.

CORRECTION | March 7, 2026: This article has been updated to reflect that of the 299 Filipino nationals aboard Emirates Flight EK336, only 23 were confirmed OFWs. The majority were stranded tourists and regular travelers. The original version did not make this distinction clear. The date of arrival has also been corrected to March 5.

🟥 FILIPINO OFWS AND THE MIDDLE EAST CRISIS: WHAT THE REPATRIATION NUMBERS ACTUALLY LOOK LIKE

The numbers are already moving. On March 5, 2026, Emirates Flight EK336 from Dubai landed at Ninoy Aquino International Airport carrying 407 passengers, 299 of them Filipino nationals. But here is the breakdown the government did not lead with: of those 299, only 23 were confirmed Overseas Filipino Workers. Two were children of OFWs. The rest were stranded tourists and regular travelers whose flights had been cancelled when Middle East airspace closed, and who boarded the first available commercial flight once it reopened. The DMW and OWWA nonetheless rolled out a full government welcome at NAIA Terminal 3, complete with financial assistance packages and a press conference, framing the arrival as a mass repatriation operation. Separately, the Philippine News Agency reported that 1,824 OFWs had been assisted since the conflict escalated, as of March 4. More flights are being planned.

Behind those numbers is a situation the Philippine government has been preparing for since at least March 1, when the United States and Israel launched coordinated strikes against Iran — designated Operation Epic Fury by the Pentagon. Iran retaliated with missile strikes toward Israel. The Department of Foreign Affairs' best estimate, delivered at a Senate hearing on March 6, is that the conflict will last four to eight weeks.

🟥 WHO IS ACTUALLY THERE

According to the Department of Migrant Workers, there were 1.113 million land-based OFWs deployed to the Middle East as of 2025 — an 11.18 percent increase over 2024. When sea-based and undocumented workers are included, the Commission on Filipinos Overseas puts the total closer to 2 million.

The concentration is uneven: 397,892 OFWs in the UAE, 386,699 in Saudi Arabia, 160,890 in Qatar, 106,364 in Kuwait, and 21,129 in Bahrain. Those five countries account for 96.4 percent of the land-based total.

Remittances from overseas Filipinos reached a record $35.63 billion in 2025 — 7.3 percent of GDP — according to Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas data. Saudi Arabia and the UAE together account for roughly 11 percent of total remittance inflows, meaning prolonged disruption to employment in either country has direct downstream effects on household spending across the Philippines.

🟥 HOW THE REPATRIATION IS RUNNING

The 299 Filipino nationals who arrived March 5 were met at NAIA by Migrant Workers Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac, with support from DMW, OWWA, the Department of Health, and the Department of Social Welfare and Development. Again, only 23 of the 299 were actual OFWs. The majority were tourists and travelers stranded by airspace closures who were already headed home once commercial flights resumed.

OWWA Administrator Patricia Yvonne Caunan disclosed that the agency's Emergency Repatriation Fund currently holds approximately P1.5 billion. The cost to repatriate a single OFW — covering airfare, welfare assistance, and logistics to their home province — runs around P150,000. At that rate, the fund could support roughly 10,000 repatriations. OWWA simulations of a mass evacuation put the total cost at P3.67 billion. DMW Secretary Cacdac told the Senate hearing that existing resources can handle current volumes, but supplemental funding would be needed if escalation continues.

The Department of Trade and Industry announced that the Small Business Corporation is opening an OFW Negosyo Fund with an initial P2 billion budget. Starting March 12, affected OFWs can apply for loans from P30,000 to P1 million, with repayment terms of up to five years and a one-year grace period on principal and interest. No collateral required for loans up to P5 million. DMW also instructed Philippine Recruitment Agencies to delay new deployments to the Middle East until the security situation stabilizes.

🟥 THE SECURITY QUESTION

Concerns circulated online about whether Iran might retaliate against the Philippines because of the Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement sites hosting US military equipment on Philippine soil. DND spokesperson Arsenio Andolong confirmed there is no credible direct threat to the Philippines from the Iran conflict, and clarified that EDCA sites are Philippine-controlled, not American bases.

Navy Rear Admiral Rommel Trinidad stated that Iran's longest-range ballistic missiles have a reach of approximately 2,000 kilometers. The Philippines sits roughly 7,500 kilometers from Iran. Vera Files fact-checked and formally debunked circulating claims that the Philippines was a target on Iran's list.

🟥 THE HISTORICAL SCALE OF IT

The Philippines has run large-scale repatriation operations before. The Gulf War of 1990 was the benchmark: when Iraq invaded Kuwait, an estimated 60,000 Filipinos were living there. The government eventually repatriated close to 30,000 OFWs from Kuwait — the largest single repatriation operation up to that point. The lessons from 1990 were absorbed into the Migrant Workers and Overseas Filipinos Act of 1995, which formalized emergency repatriation procedures and mandated a dedicated Emergency Repatriation Fund.

The 2006 Lebanon conflict tested the system again, with around 30,000 Filipinos in the country when fighting broke out. More than 10,000 OFWs returned from Lebanon, Egypt, Yemen, Libya, and Syria combined during the Arab Spring of 2011.

Each crisis followed the same pattern: initial shock, a surge of repatriation requests, a race between logistics and funding, and then the question of what happens to workers who come back without jobs waiting for them.

🟥 WHERE THE NUMBERS STAND

As of early March 2026, over 1.1 million documented land-based OFWs are in the Middle East. OWWA recorded 1,189 formal requests by March 4, broken down as 278 from Israel, 62 from Dubai, 246 from Abu Dhabi, and others from Qatar and Lebanon. The DFA is projecting four to eight weeks of active conflict.

The government's current position is that OFWs should shelter in place unless they are in active danger — moving large numbers through a conflict zone may carry higher risk than remaining in a secure facility. Whether the institutional depth built up since 1990 is sufficient for a conflict involving over a million workers and a two-month timeline is the question the next few weeks will answer.

🟥 SARA DUTERTE RELEASES VIDEO MESSAGE ON OIL CRISIS, TELLS GOVERNMENT AGENCIES TO ACT NOW ON FUEL AND FOOD PRICES

BREAKING | March 8, 2026

While facing impeachment proceedings in the House, Vice President Sara Duterte shifted to economic messaging on Saturday and released a video statement aimed at government agencies, local governments, and Filipino families.

The subject: the fuel price crisis.

Her ask: immediate government intervention on prices, a halt to non-essential fuel-consuming government activities, the creation of commodity price monitoring committees at the local level, and direct cash aid for low-income households.

🟥 WHAT SHE SAID

The video was posted on Duterte's official Facebook page on March 7. She spoke in Filipino and addressed multiple audiences — national government agencies, local government units, and ordinary citizens.

To government agencies, her message was direct: "Immediate action is required. We must closely monitor fuel prices and their repercussions on essential goods and transportation."

She called for programs to prevent hoarding and to stop speculative pricing in the oil market.

She pushed for a committee — at the national and local levels — to track oil prices, fare rates, and other basic commodities.

She proposed temporary price caps on food items.

She urged the government to consider targeted cash transfers for low-income families.

She asked for advisories on fuel-efficient food distribution and incentives for public transportation.

🟥 THE LGU INSTRUCTIONS

A notable section of the video was aimed specifically at local government units.

Duterte told LGUs to temporarily ban non-essential activities that consume fuel — naming festival parades specifically.

She asked them to form committees that will "keep an eye on prices of basic commodities."

She urged LGUs to boost support for local farmers and suppliers, framing it as a way to reduce reliance on imports and cushion the price shock at the community level.

🟥 THE HOUSEHOLD ADVICE

Duterte also addressed Filipino families directly, offering household-level recommendations.

Buy local products to strengthen the domestic economy. Cut unnecessary energy use. Carpool. Use bicycles. Adjust schedules to reduce travel frequency.

"We can find ways to lighten our burden — by working together in our communities, supporting local products, and being mindful of our spending and energy use," she said.

🟥 THE CONTEXT AROUND THE VIDEO

The timing is loaded.

This video dropped one day after the Department of Energy warned that diesel prices could exceed P80 per liter this month if oil companies implement the full price adjustment without staggering.

Oil industry sources cited by GMA News project gasoline will rise by P7.09 per liter and diesel by P15.33 per liter when the next round of adjustments takes effect on March 10.

These are not routine increases. Year-to-date through March 3, gasoline prices had already climbed P6.70 per liter, diesel by P9.40, and kerosene by P7.70. The projected March 10 adjustment would nearly double the diesel increase in a single week.

The root cause remains the US-Iran conflict and the resulting disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. China has suspended new refined fuel export contracts, squeezing supply for importing nations like the Philippines.

🟥 THE POLITICAL BACKDROP

Duterte released this video while her impeachment case moves forward in the House of Representatives. The Committee on Justice voted 54-1 on March 4 to declare two impeachment complaints sufficient in substance. She has 10 days to respond.

On the same night she released this fuel price video, Duterte also appeared on a web series produced by her office where she accused President Marcos of lying to her about the impeachment plan — a separate, sharper confrontation that dominated Friday evening's political news cycle.

The oil crisis video, by contrast, contained no mention of the impeachment or any political rival. It focused entirely on fuel prices, commodity monitoring, and household budgets.

The Marcos administration had already moved on the fuel crisis earlier in the week. President Marcos ordered a temporary four-day workweek for executive branch offices starting March 9 and asked Congress for emergency powers to cut fuel excise taxes. Palace Press Officer Claire Castro, when asked about Duterte's earlier impeachment-related statements, said: "This is not the time for politicking or self-promotion."

Neither the Palace nor any Cabinet member had publicly responded to Duterte's oil price video as of Saturday evening.

🟥 SARA DUTERTE SAYS MARCOS LIED TO HER FACE ABOUT THE IMPEACHMENT, THEN WHISPERED "I'LL TAKE CARE OF YOU"

UPDATE | March 8, 2026

Vice President Sara Duterte went on camera Friday night and accused President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. of lying to her directly when she confronted him about impeachment plans in November 2023.

The accusation came during an episode of a web series produced by the Office of the Vice President, broadcast on Duterte's social media accounts late Friday.

🟥 THE CONFRONTATION

According to Duterte, she learned in late 2023 that members of the House of Representatives had been planning to impeach her over her alleged misuse of confidential funds. She wanted answers.

She contacted Senator Imee Marcos, the President's sister, to arrange a meeting. Duterte said she was "not friends" with the President despite their 2022 joint campaign.

The day before Marcos left for the APEC Summit in San Francisco, the three met.

Duterte said she confronted Marcos directly and asked why impeachment was being planned against her in the House. His response, according to her: "Oh that's not true. There's nothing like that."

But then, she said, he talked too much.

"His denial stopped because he talked too much. It came out of his mouth that the priority was Charter change."

Senator Imee Marcos, who was present, reportedly questioned her brother about Charter change. The President replied it was about "economic provisions."

🟥 "I'LL TAKE CARE OF YOU"

The next day, Duterte said she was part of the group that bid farewell to Marcos before his flight. As he stood on the aircraft stairs, she said, Marcos approached her and whispered: "Don't worry, I'll take care of you."

Duterte's interpretation was blunt.

"I was surprised. You lied last night that you didn't know about the impeachment. Why would you say 'I'll take care of you'?"

She pressed the point harder: "We were together for so long in the campaign. The heat, the dehydration, the exhaustion. He never said 'I'll take care of you.' Then suddenly I confront him about the impeachment and he whispers it to me before he leaves? Why do you need to take care of me? You know something, right?"

When asked directly if Marcos had a hand in the impeachment planning, she said: "He has a hand in it. Why would you deny it then say 'I'll take care of you'? He probably didn't think his lying would be caught by his own words the next day."

🟥 ROMUALDEZ, CHARTER CHANGE, AND THE BIGGER ACCUSATION

Duterte went further. She claimed the Marcos administration was positioning former House Speaker Martin Romualdez as the next president and that allies were pushing Charter change through a people's initiative.

She alleged the Department of Labor and Employment's TUPAD program was used to gather signatures for Charter change, and that the scheme collapsed after public backlash.

She also claimed there were discussions to abolish the Office of the Vice President entirely and extend the terms of House members.

GMA News requested comment from Romualdez. He had not responded as of broadcast time.

🟥 THE PALACE RESPONSE

Palace Press Officer Claire Castro dismissed the timing of Duterte's statements.

"This is not the time for politicking or self-promotion. The President is currently busy with the voice of the Philippines crying out for peace and security. Our President is also busy alleviating the impact of the Middle East situation on our country and people."

Castro did not address the specific allegations about the November 2023 confrontation.

🟥 WHERE THIS FITS

This comes days after the House Committee on Justice voted 54-1 to declare two impeachment complaints against Duterte sufficient in substance. Duterte has 10 days to respond. The case is now in the pleadings stage.

If the full House votes to impeach, the Senate becomes the trial court. Conviction requires 16 of 24 senators and would permanently bar Duterte from holding public office.

Duterte is widely seen as a front-runner for the 2028 presidential election. A conviction would end that.

🟥 ANNE CURTIS REJECTS CONGRESSMAN'S NON-APOLOGY AFTER LEWD REMARKS IN CONGRESS, LEGAL ACTION ON THE TABLE

DEVELOPING | March 7, 2026

Actress Anne Curtis broke her silence on Saturday, rejecting the apology of Quezon City Representative Bong Suntay after he publicly described having sexual desire for her during a formal House hearing.

"I do not accept your non-apology," Curtis said on social media.

She explained she had delayed her response because her family had been stranded in the Middle East. Now home, she was ready to speak — "not for revenge or drama, but because remaining silent would be wrong... for me, and for every woman watching."

🟥 WHAT SUNTAY SAID

On March 3, during House Committee on Justice deliberations on the impeachment complaints against Vice President Sara Duterte, Suntay argued that Duterte's past public statements were merely imagination and not impeachable. He used Curtis as an example.

He recounted seeing Curtis at a hotel, said "a desire inside me welled up," and described imagining "what could happen" — arguing he could not be charged for his imagination. The full quote, in the original Tagalog, was captured on video and broadcast nationally.

San Juan Representative Ysabel Zamora immediately objected. The committee voted 13-2 to strike the remarks from the record. Suntay protested, calling the vote a "dangerous precedent."

Notable context: Suntay was the sole dissenting vote — 54 to 1 — when the same committee declared the Duterte impeachment complaints sufficient in substance the following day. He is a Duterte ally endorsed by the Vice President in 2025.

🟥 CURTIS FIRES BACK

Curtis described the remarks as "vulgar and sexualized," her name used "without my consent... and without an ounce of respect."

"What happened to me isn't rare. It happens to women every day... in offices, in group chats, in rooms where men think no one is listening, or worse, in rooms where they know everyone is and simply don't care."

She addressed Suntay directly: "Since you used me as an example, let me use you as one too. You've become the poster boy of something much bigger: a culture that still thinks it's acceptable to talk about women this way."

"You hold a seat paid for by taxpayers. Women are taxpayers. I am a taxpayer. We are not props in your commentary."

She accepted the separate apology issued by Suntay's wife, Sheila Guevara-Suntay, who publicly condemned her husband's remarks on March 5. She called on the House ethics committee to act and confirmed she is seeking legal advice.

🟥 LEGAL AND INSTITUTIONAL FALLOUT

On March 4, the House plenary ordered Suntay to face the Committee on Ethics, authorizing an immediate hearing.

On March 6, nine women — including members of the World March of Women-Pilipinas — filed a complaint-affidavit at the Office of the Ombudsman. They cited violations of the Safe Spaces Act, the Magna Carta of Women, and the Code of Conduct for Public Officials. Because Suntay is a practicing lawyer, the complaint also raised the Code of Professional Responsibility.

The complainants called his statements a "declaration of predatory desire" and accused him of "gaslighting" with his conditional apology.

The Philippine Commission on Women, a government agency, labeled the remarks a "public act of sexual objectification." Anne Curtis' legal counsel called them "clearly inappropriate and unacceptable."

Suntay maintained his remarks carried no malice, pointing to his record authoring Quezon City's Gender and Development Code and anti-harassment ordinance.

🟥 ESTRADA ADDS ANOTHER INCIDENT

On March 6, Senator Jinggoy Estrada drew separate backlash during a Senate hearing on the naturalization of American basketball player Elizabeth Means for the Gilas Pilipinas Women's team.

Estrada asked her: "Do you have a Filipino boyfriend?"

After Means replied "Not yet po," Estrada suggested Senator Francis Pangilinan "might want to apply" — a joke referencing Pangilinan's wife, Sharon Cuneta.

Estrada dismissed the criticism as "friendly banter." Unlike the Suntay incident, no formal complaints have been filed. But two male lawmakers objectifying two women in two separate hearings — all during the first week of Women's Month — drew a unified public response.

As Curtis put it: "We deserve better. Not just now, while this topic is a hot issue, but every single day."
